system_prompt:
You are writing the "statement of outputs" — a human-readable narrative that
synthesizes everything the book teaches into a coherent description of the master/work's method.

The user will give you the book-level distillation, the chapter summaries, and
the structured systems-draft JSON you just derived. Write the statement.

Structure:

  # <Work Title> — Statement of Outputs

  ## Overview
  (2-3 paragraphs on what the book is and what it teaches, citing the
  book-level distillation.)

  ## Systems
  For each system in the systems-draft, one ## section. For each:
    - one paragraph describing the system in plain language
    - the system's members listed
    - the traversal rules and modification rules listed with their
      engine_payload kinds shown in code formatting
    - citations back to the relevant chapter summaries that backed each
      claim (e.g. "(Chapter 2 summary; ch02.md quotes)")

  ## Pending Work
  List every `_pending:<kebab>` engine_payload.kind value with what it
  signals. If there are none, omit this section.

  ## Provenance Notes
  Brief: what's drawn from what. Note any chapters that didn't yield a
  system and why.

This is the human-facing artifact. Be precise, cite, and don't invent
content outside the summaries and quote files.

# Book-level distillation — goldmine-100-jazz-lessons

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

This guitar method builds a complete jazz language from the ground up, organized around a core harmonic cell and progressively more sophisticated ways of navigating it. Chapter 1 establishes the foundational posture: comping is not mechanical strumming but variable rhythmic accompaniment built on upbeat emphasis and chord anticipation, and even single-scale melodic lines can imply harmonic motion through strategic targeting of chord tones. Chapter 2 formalizes the ii-V-I as the irreducible harmonic cell of jazz, fixing the chord qualities (minor seven, dominant seven, major seven) and the voice-leading rule that each chord's 7th resolves to the next chord's 3rd. Everything that follows is an expansion, complication, or creative escape from this cell.

The book's central harmonic teaching unfolds across Chapters 3, 6, and 11. Tritone substitution — replacing a chord with one rooted a tritone away, justified by the shared tones at the b5 pivot — is introduced in Chapter 3 as both a compositional tool and an improvisational option available independently of the stated changes. Chapter 6 deepens this with scale-degree equivalences: the 7sus4 chord equals a minor seventh built on the dominant's 5th; Lydian dominant generates maj7#11 via stacked thirds; Bb melodic minor unifies multiple chord types. Chapter 11 systematizes the altered dominant territory through the four tension tones (#5, b5, b9, #9) and three co-dependent scale systems — the diminished (half-step/whole-step formula, with three-fret equivalence covering four keys), harmonic minor, and melodic minor — while identifying Super Locrian (the seventh mode of melodic minor) as the master tool that captures all altered tones and links diminished harmony to dominant seventh voicings.

Scale choice over dominant seventh chords is the book's recurring battleground. Chapter 2 establishes Dorian, Mixolydian, and major for major-key ii-V-I, and Locrian, altered dominant, and Dorian for minor. Chapter 4 introduces the key decision rule: minor ii-V progressions strongly favor altered dominants, and a common jazz device shifts a dominant from Mixolydian (natural) to super Locrian (altered) immediately before resolution to create urgency. Chapter 10 names the bebop dominant scale as Charlie Parker's preferred tool and the diminished scale as a high-demand specialty, while warning that both resist overuse. Throughout, the author presses a strategic reduction method: group chords by key and apply a single scale across related chord sections, ignoring brief non-diatonic chords or emphasizing their chord tones only as needed.

Melodic construction is taught through a succession of named techniques and prescriptive exercises. Chapter 4's Constant Phrase Shape fixes a rhythmic figure and varies pitch, while Changing Rhythmic Shape takes a row of selected tones and reshapes them rhythmically using swung eighths, slides, and triplets — establishing the principle that a musical idea is a composite of tones and timing, and that phrase shape is an improvisation mindset rather than a formula. Chapter 5 introduces Pattern 1 (descending groups of four) and Pattern 2 (directional change within groupings), plus "the Lick" defined by the scale-degree formula 1–2–3–4–2–7–1. Chapter 7 adds hemiola (metric contrast within a fixed time signature applied to blues-scale figures), the half-step outside device (a major-seventh chord a half-step above, justified by tritone-substitution logic), and one-tone harmonization (a single note harmonized across an entire progression). Chapter 9 introduces the seven-eighth-note shifting drill, the one-string three-note permutation system, and the three-octave string-group structure for ascending lines. Chapter 12 codifies neighbor notes (half-step above or below any scale tone, essential in jazz, used sparingly in blues), enclosure targeting (surrounding a destination note to create the bebop sound), and interval jumping with riffs at half-step, whole-step, or 3rd transpositions.

Comping and voicing systems receive equal systematic treatment alongside the melodic material. Chapter 5's shell voicings distill chords to root, 3rd, and 7th — then eliminate the root for compact two-string shapes on middle strings — and demonstrate that tritone substitutes share shell shapes with inverted 3rds and 7ths. Chapter 8 introduces quartal harmony (built on guitar's native perfect-4th string intervals), rootless voicings, and the explicit warning that scales are starting points, not methods: they liberate initially but trap the player in boxes if mistaken for complete note-choice systems. Chapter 13 systematizes chord mastery through dual voicings (root on sixth or fifth string), movable shapes, and intervallic formulas (major = 1-3-5; minor = 1-b3-5; dominant = 1-3-5-b7), and names the three cardinal chord-melody techniques: melody note always on top, melody within a held chord shape, and Jazz 101 family substitution.

Rhythm and feel are treated as a parallel technical language rather than a byproduct of note choice. Chapter 1's chord anticipation — placing chords one eighth-note early to account for swing timing — is the earliest rhythmic prescription and defines the jazz comping posture. Chapter 5 specifies that the Freddie Green straight-4 style requires precise release timing, minimal voicing size, and low dynamic placement ("felt more than heard"). Chapter 8 names three swing-feel techniques — playing behind the beat, playing straight 8ths, and accenting upbeats — and distinguishes feel variation from rhythmic variation as separate dimensions. Ballad feel is defined specifically as a mixture of even 8ths and triplets with arpeggiated rather than struck chord attacks. Chapter 4 enforces a stylistic boundary rule: extreme blues bending and distortion are incompatible with jazz vocabulary acquisition, and the prescribed remedy is suppressing blues gestures until jazz tonal and rhythmic choices are internalized.

The book's pedagogical architecture is a deliberate one-way gate: master the cell before its substitutes, master chord tones before scales, master scales before recognizing their limits. Chapter 2 frames chord-tone-only improvisation as a stepping stone rather than an endpoint. Chapter 8 names the trap explicitly — scales free the player initially but become boxes — and identifies comping density (sparse guide tones, simple pentatonic ideas over complex changes, or laying out entirely) as the primary dynamic lever in musical conversation. Chapter 10's revelation that multiple pentatonic scales are hidden within one major scale, and that chord shapes function as melodic skeletons disguising scale patterns, represents the book's mature synthesis: fretboard geometry, harmonic function, and rhythmic inflection are not separate subjects but three descriptions of the same underlying jazz logic.

# Chapter 1 — Comping Fundamentals and Turnarounds (Lessons 1-2)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

Chapter 1 introduces comping as the foundation of jazz accompaniment, defined as variable rhythmic and harmonic accompaniment distinct from mechanical strumming, with two signature devices: emphasis on upbeats (the "ands" in counted measures) and chord anticipation (playing chords one eighth-note early to create forward momentum while accounting for swing timing). The chapter teaches a pedagogical progression from locked rhythmic figures repeated through chord changes, to understanding the turnaround—a specific harmonic formula (typically I-VI-ii-V-I, exemplified as Bbmaj6-G7-Cm7-F7-Bbmaj6) that returns the form to its top—and then three graduated approaches to improvising over changes: scale-based lines using only the home key's tones while targeting chord-tone contours, chord-tone-only arpeggio lines through the ninth with explicit extensions, and per-chord scale selection (using altered or super Locrian for altered dominants). The master's characteristic move is the compositional principle that even single-scale lines can imply harmonic motion through strategic targeting of underlying chord tones. The core terminology load-bearing this chapter includes comping itself, upbeat emphasis, chord anticipation, turnaround, arpeggio-based improvisation, and altered scales, all organized around the key insight that jazz rhythm and harmony live in constant dialogue between fixed changes and flexible phrasing.

# Chapter 2 — II-V-I Cells and Blues Comping (Lessons 3-6)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 teaches the ii-V-I progression as a foundational harmonic cell, establishing the chord qualities (minor seven for ii, dominant seven for V, major seven for I) and voice-leading principle that the 7th of each chord resolves to the 3rd of the next. The chapter introduces both major and minor ii-V-i variants, with their associated scales (Dorian, Mixolydian, and major for major keys; Locrian, altered dominant, and Dorian for minor), and addresses how ii-V-I cells may tonicize chords other than the tonic. A central pedagogical move is the chord-tone-only improvisation exercise, which maps chord members across the fretboard while developing harmonic ear, framed as a stepping stone rather than an end goal. The chapter also presents jazz blues comping as a distinct form from rock blues, defined by ii-V substitutions and the principle that written chord progressions function as harmonic frameworks rather than rigid prescriptions. Key terminology introduced includes Super Locrian scale for altered dominants, half-diminished for the minor ii chord, and the concept of tonicization within progressions.

# Chapter 3 — Tritone Substitution and Minor Blues (Lessons 7-10)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 teaches tritone substitution—replacing a chord with one rooted a tritone away—as a device for creating chromatic bass movement and altered dominant sounds, particularly through the theoretical pivot of the b5 chord where dominant and substitute meet. The instruction covers extending this concept from single chords to entire ii-V units in bebop harmony, and applying it to turnarounds where parallel chromatic voicing preserves harmonic function while changing sonic character. The chapter then shifts to minor blues structure, where the i and iv chords become minor while the V remains dominant, often preceded by a natural dominant bVI that leads to an altered V—establishing a stylistic rule for dominant chord selection. Load-bearing techniques include the 5-3-2-1 scalar descent for melodic lines over tritone substitutions and the "aim for the change" strategy of targeting chord-defining tones like the half-step 3rd shift (B-flat to B natural in a Gm to G7 change) to emphasize harmonic movement. The chapter emphasizes that soloists can imply substitute harmony independently of the stated changes, making tritone substitution both a compositional tool and an improvisational option.

# Chapter 4 — Phrase Shape, Call-Response, Altered Sounds (Lessons 11-17)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 introduces two core improvisational methods: Constant Phrase Shape, which fixes a rhythmic figure and varies pitch within it to sharpen harmonic listening, and Changing Rhythmic Shape, which takes a row of selected tones and reshapes them rhythmically using tools like swung eighths, slides, and triplets. The chapter establishes that a musical idea is fundamentally a composite of tones and timing, governed by phrase shape as an improvisation mindset. It presents structural rules for Call and Response (requiring symmetrical form), the "skating" technique of moving blues scale material through chord changes, and a critical taxonomy of altered dominant decisions: minor ii-V progressions strongly favor altered dominants, while a common jazz device shifts a dominant from natural to altered before resolution to create urgency. The scalar mechanism for this natural-to-altered shift involves switching from Mixolydian to super Locrian (altered) scales, though the chapter identifies gaps in standard scalar theory where no single scale fully supplies required tones. A stylistic boundary rule warns against extreme blues bending and distortion in jazz contexts, introducing devices like "reverse swing" rhythms, while a prescriptive pedagogical stance counsels suppressing blues gestures to focus on acquiring jazz vocabulary through tonal and rhythmic choice.

# Chapter 5 — 3/4 Time, Picking, and Chordal Shells (Lessons 18-25)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

Chapter 5 teaches rhythmic and harmonic foundations for jazz guitar in 3/4 time and straight-4 swing, establishing that in 3/4 measures the "and" of beat 2 receives emphasis and (by default) a second chord entry, while solo accompaniment uses a bass-and-chords figure combining distinct rhythmic layers. The chapter introduces core left-hand techniques—finger rolls (both ascending on the tip and descending with flat pre-positioning) and finger substitution following a directional rule—alongside right-hand picking discipline: rhythm-governed alternate picking maintains continuous motion to catch downbeats and upbeats, while straight-4 quarter notes require precise release timing (evoking Freddie Green's swing phrasing), minimal voicing size, and low dynamic placement ("felt more than heard"). Shell voicings, the chapter's load-bearing harmonic concept, distill chords to their defining tones (root, 3rd, 7th), then eliminate the root to create compact two-string shapes on the middle strings that outline progressions alongside a walking bass—a technique reinforced by observing that tritone substitutes share shell shapes with inverted 3rds and 7ths. Scalar practice patterns (Pattern 1's descending groups of four, Pattern 2's directional change within groupings) and the scale-degree formula for "the Lick" (1–2–3–4–2–7–1) round out the chapter's melodic toolkit, emphasizing interval-driven versatility across scales and chords.

# Chapter 6 — Extensions, Bossa, Octaves, Chromatics (Lessons 26-33)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

Chapter 6 teaches advanced jazz guitar voicing and soloing strategies by mapping scale degrees to chord extensions, starting with deriving minor seventh and dominant chords from their parent scales (Dorian and Lydian dominant respectively). The signature improvisational moves include soloing over Dm7 using Am9 arpeggio from the 5th up, employing B minor pentatonic on Cmaj7#11 to avoid dissonance with the tonic and 5th, and substituting Dm7 ideas over G7sus4 to access upper extensions. Key techniques introduced include octave fingerings with muted strings for ergonomic vertical neck movement, chromatic enclosure where strong beats land on basic chord tones, and the Bossa nova fingerstyle method using thumb for bass and fingers for muted chording. The chapter establishes critical equivalences—that the Lydian scale generates maj7#11 via stacked thirds, that 7sus4 equals a minor seventh chord built on its 5th, and that Bb melodic minor unifies multiple chord types—while introducing load-bearing terminology like altered (super Locrian) scale, Lydian dominant, and the jazz-specific unresolved 7sus4 voicing that does not revert to the major 3rd.

# Chapter 7 — Sequences, Substitutions, Harmonization (Lessons 34-42)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

Chapter 7 teaches sequential practice strategies for ii-V progressions and introduces hemiola as a rhythmic device for creating metric contrast within a fixed time signature, with specific mechanical rules for applying hemiola patterns to blues scale figures. The chapter covers tritone substitution as a three-step method for altering dominant chords before resolution, and presents harmonization techniques including the one-tone harmonization exercise where a single note is harmonized across an entire chord progression, and double-stop intervals (particularly 3rds and 4ths) as the physically natural choice on guitar. Transposition is anchored through fretboard signposts—the 12th fret octave and the 5th/4th fret unison points—while slash chord notation (e.g., D/G) designates the bass note rather than implying a voicing patch of the default shape. The chapter also introduces the half-step outside device as a brief excursion away from the chord zone, justified through tritone-substitution logic where a major-seventh chord a half-step above can replicate the resolution effect of a V-I progression. Load-bearing terminology includes hemiola, tritone substitute, slash chord, and the one-tone harmonization method, which together form a toolkit for constructing melodic lines and navigating harmonic space.

# Chapter 8 — Quartals, Inner-Voice Motion, Scales and Comping Texture (Lessons 43-50)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch08.md`._

Chapter 8 introduces quartal harmony, inner-voice motion, and comping texture as tools for advancing beyond scalar thinking in jazz guitar. The physics of quartal playing on guitar—built on the perfect 4ths between most string pairs—enables outside patterns and requires finger-rolling technique, while rootless voicings and sus4 devices create melodic motion within chord changes. The chapter warns explicitly that scales are starting points, not methods: they free the player initially but trap them in boxes if mistaken for complete note-choice systems, a distinction the author marks as central to improvisation beyond mechanical chord-tone selection. Comping density emerges as a primary dynamic lever—sparse guide-tone textures, simple pentatonic ideas layered over complex changes, and the choice to lay out entirely all shape intensity and musical conversation. Three named swing-feel techniques (playing behind the beat, playing straight 8ths, accenting upbeats) and the distinction between feel variation and rhythmic variation equip the player to inflect the same written figure in multiple ways. Ballad feel is defined by a specific mix of even 8ths and triplets, paired with arpeggiated (rather than struck) chord attacks—a textural shift that respects the slower harmonic rhythm of ballad forms.

# Chapter 9 — Concept, Shifting, String-Centric Lines, Arpeggios (Lessons 51-58)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch09.md`._

Chapter 9 teaches creative approaches to scalar and linear motion through numeric pattern sequencing, position shifting, and string-specific techniques. The governing principle is breaking away from straight scalar motion—achieved through exercises like the seven-eighth-note shifting drill (which pairs quick thinking under time pressure with chord-change navigation) and the one-string thing's three-note permutation system. Key techniques include alternate picking for single-string speed, sliding with a light grip for fluency, and the foundational sweep-picking approach to arpeggios. The chapter introduces the three-octave string-group system (dividing low, middle, and high strings to structure long ascending lines with repeating fingering patterns) and mini-barre-mini-sweep technique for pick-based playing. Diatonic arpeggio construction—breaking scale tones into 1-3-5 triads and add9/slash-9 extensions—escapes linear scalar sound, while legato triplets demand pinky strength and anticipatory thinking. Throughout, the master emphasizes listening for sound over fingering convenience and developing a light-grip shifting technique that enables fluid hand movement across the neck.

# Chapter 10 — Scales, Modes, Soloing Strategies (Lessons 59-70)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch10.md`._

Chapter 10 teaches scale sequencing through fretboard fingering positions as the foundation for constructing jazz lines quickly, then pivots to modes—where a single set of notes (like the C major scale) creates different sounds depending on the background chord or bass note, exemplified by the popular Spanish sound of E Phrygian. The chapter introduces a strategic reduction method: grouping chords by key to apply single scales across related chord progressions, with a two-part rule for non-diatonic chords that appear briefly (ignore them or emphasize their chord tones as needed). A characteristic teaching move is the revelation of multiple pentatonic scales hidden within one major scale, and the concept of chord shapes as melodic skeletons that disguise scale patterns. The author emphasizes practical scale-shifting techniques—chromatic riff-shifting, the minor-third rule to convert major to minor, and interval-practice methods that treat scale transposition like barre chord shifts. The dominant seventh chord emerges as the central battleground for scale choice in jazz, where options proliferate once the b7th is introduced, leading to specialized tools like the bebop dominant scale (favored by Charlie Parker) and the diminished scale, both of which demand mastery while resisting overuse.

# Chapter 11 — Dissonance, Chromatic Riffs, Diminished and Altered Minor (Lessons 71-79)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch11.md`._

Chapter 11 teaches dissonance and chromatic techniques built on the tritone substitution principle—chords on the b5 share tones with the original—and introduces the four tension tones (#5, b5, b9, #9) that distinguish altered dominants from the native scale. The chapter systematizes three key scales: the diminished scale (half-step/whole-step formula) with its three-fret equivalence covering four keys, the harmonic minor scale overlaid on diminished chords, and the melodic minor scale (major with flat 3) with its seventh-chord harmonization. The master's signature move is chromatic displacement—ascending or descending riffs moved by half-steps or whole steps up the fretboard—requiring careful resolution back to in-key targets to create both tension and coherence. Super Locrian mode, the seventh degree of melodic minor, captures all altered tones and connects diminished harmony to dominant seventh voicings without the perfect fifth. Throughout, the chapter emphasizes scale-over-chord equivalences: diminished scales work over their generated seventh chords, harmonic minor over diminished triads found within it, and the enharmonic relationship between G super Locrian and Ab melodic minor.

# Chapter 12 — Neighbors, Pentatonic Mixing, Geometric and Interval Lines (Lessons 80-89)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch12.md`._

Chapter 12 teaches foundational soloing techniques built on neighbor notes, pentatonic mixing, and intervallic design. Half-step neighbors—notes one fret above or below any scale tone—form the basis for jazz improvisation (essential) and blues playing (use sparingly), while pentatonic mixing exploits five overlapping pentatonic positions from different keys available simultaneously on the fretboard. Geometric patterns and sweep picking establish aural internalization of shapes before theoretical analysis, with clean articulation and finger release ensuring clarity in string-skipping lines built on 6th intervals. The chapter introduces interval jumping with riffs (repeating lines at half-step, whole-step, or 3rd intervals), whole-tone harmony (only two mutually exclusive scales covering the entire system), and targeting through enclosure—arranging notes around a chosen destination to create direction and the bebop sound characteristic of jazz. A recurring master move is selective attention: jazz musicians need not play every chord change but can target chord tones across fast tempos, and when jamming in one key, nearby keys with only one-note difference (such as A, D, and E) remain compatible for free expression.

# Chapter 13 — Chord Systems, Voicings, and Standards Interpretation (Lessons 90-100)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch13.md`._

Chapter 13 systematizes chord mastery through three organizing principles: dual voicings (root on sixth or fifth string to minimize fretboard movement), movable shapes (transposable across all twelve keys when not using open strings), and formula-based construction (modifying intervals within the three chord families—major, minor, and dominant—to generate vocabulary). The author teaches intervallic formulas (major triad = 1-3-5; minor = 1-b3-5; dominant = 1-3-5-b7) and introduces partial voicings and rootless chords to expand harmonic possibility. Three cardinal techniques emerge: chord melody (melody note always on top), melody within the chord (picking notes inside a held shape), and Jazz 101 substitution (exchanging chords within the same family). The circle of fourths and five standard chord-change intervals (half step, minor third up/down, fourth, fifth) provide navigation rules, while the distinction between diatonic changes (chords within the key) and root-interval movements (4ths, 5ths, stepwise) enables both structured and free chord choice.

          "id": "i-maj7",
          "name": "I \u2014 Major Seventh",

          "name": "i \u2014 Minor Seventh (minor tonic)",
          "summary": "Minor tonic in minor ii-V-i; Dorian scale."
        },
        {

          "id": "v7sus4",
          "name": "V7sus4 \u2014 Suspended Dominant",

        }

    {
      "id": "heussenstamm-silbergleit:goldmine-100-jazz-lessons:scale-over-chord-selection",
      "name": "Scale-over-Chord Selection and Reduction System",
      "summary": "This system governs which scale a player deploys over any given chord type. Members are the principal scale/mode options arranged around the dominant seventh as the central battleground: Mixolydian (natural), Lydian dominant (raised 4th), super Locrian / altered (seventh mode of melodic minor), diminished (half-step/whole-step octatonic), bebop dominant, harmonic minor (over diminished-function chords), and pentatonic options. Traversal rules include the strategic reduction principle (group chords by key; apply one scale across a region, ignoring brief non-diatonic chords or emphasizing their chord tones), the three-fret equivalence rule for the diminished scale (one fingering covers four keys), and the pentatonic-within-diatonic revelation (four pentatonics hidden in a single major scale). Modification rules include the Lydian-dominant avoidance of the natural 4th, the super Locrian / Ab melodic minor enharmonic identity, and the explicit ceiling on scale-as-method thinking (scales are starting points that liberate initially but trap the player in boxes if mistaken for complete note-choice systems).",
      "members": [
        {
          "id": "dorian",
          "name": "Dorian \u2014 Minor Seventh / ii Chord Scale",
          "summary": "Natural minor with raised 6th; used over ii min7 and minor tonic in minor ii-V-i."
        },
        {
          "id": "mixolydian",
          "name": "Mixolydian \u2014 Natural Dominant Scale",
          "summary": "Major scale with flat 7th; default choice for natural dominant seventh."
        },
        {
          "id": "lydian-dominant",
          "name": "Lydian Dominant \u2014 Unaltered Dominant with #11",
          "summary": "Mixolydian with raised 4th; avoids clash between natural 4th and chordal 3rd; accommodates #11."
        },
        {
          "id": "super-locrian-altered",
          "name": "Super Locrian / Altered Scale",
          "summary": "Seventh mode of melodic minor; captures all four tension tones; enharmonically identical to melodic minor built a half step above the altered dominant root."
        },
        {
          "id": "diminished-hw",
          "name": "Diminished Scale (Half-Step / Whole-Step)",
          "summary": "Octatonic scale with H-W alternation; covers four keys per fingering (three-fret equivalence)."
        },
        {
          "id": "bebop-dominant",
          "name": "Bebop Dominant Scale",
          "summary": "Mixolydian plus natural major 7th passing tone; eight-note scale favored by Charlie Parker."
        },
        {
          "id": "harmonic-minor",
          "name": "Harmonic Minor",
          "summary": "Natural minor with raised 7th; applicable over diminished chords found within it."
        },
        {
          "id": "melodic-minor",
          "name": "Melodic Minor (Jazz)",
          "summary": "Major scale with flat 3rd; parent of super Locrian, Lydian dominant, and multiple chord types via harmonization."
        },
        {
          "id": "pentatonic-minor",
          "name": "Minor Pentatonic / Pentatonic Mixing",
          "summary": "Five-note subset; up to four pentatonic scales hidden within one major scale; overlapping positions usable simultaneously."
        },
        {
          "id": "whole-tone",
          "name": "Whole Tone Scale",
          "summary": "Only two mutually exclusive scales cover the entire chromatic system; generates augmented and #5 dominant chord sounds."
        },
        {
          "id": "locrian",
          "name": "Locrian \u2014 Half-Diminished / iim7b5 Scale",
          "summary": "Used over the half-diminished ii chord in minor ii-V-i contexts."
        }
      ],
      "traversal_rules": [
        {
          "id": "chord-grouping-key-reduction",
          "name": "Group Chords by Key \u2014 Single-Scale Region Reduction",
          "summary": "Analyze a tune to group chords into key regions; apply one scale across the entire region rather than switching scale per chord.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "PositionContinuity",
            "mechanism": "key_region_grouping"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 10,
              "topic": "Grouping chords by key for soloing",
              "quote_excerpt": "we only need to apply one scale to groups of chords that are related to one key",
              "page": 124
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "non-diatonic-chord-handling",
          "name": "Non-Diatonic Chord Two-Part Rule",
          "summary": "For briefly appearing non-diatonic chords, either ignore them and continue the key-region scale, or emphasize their chord tones as added color.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "NCTHarmonization",
            "options": [
              "ignore_brief",
              "emphasize_chord_tones"
            ]
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 10,
              "topic": "Non-diatonic chord handling",
              "quote_excerpt": "outside-the-key chords come and go quickly, you can choose to not pay attention to them",
              "page": 125
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "diminished-three-fret-equivalence",
          "name": "Diminished Scale Three-Fret Equivalence",
          "summary": "One diminished scale fingering pattern repeats every three frets and covers four keys simultaneously.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "SymmetryMovement",
            "interval_frets": 3,
            "keys_covered": 4
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 11,
              "topic": "Diminished scale three-fret equivalence",
              "quote_excerpt": "same exact patterns and fingerings occur three frets above and below",
              "page": 152
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "pentatonics-within-diatonic",
          "name": "Four Pentatonics Hidden Within One Major Scale",
          "summary": "A single diatonic major scale contains three (or four, with #4/b5) minor pentatonic scales usable simultaneously over related chords.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "FamilyCoherence",
            "derived_members": [
              "pentatonic-minor"
            ],
            "count": 4
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 10,
              "topic": "Pentatonics within diatonic major",
              "quote_excerpt": "C diatonic major scale contains three minor pentatonic scales within it",
              "page": 126
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "super-locrian-melodic-minor-identity",
          "name": "Super Locrian / Ab Melodic Minor Enharmonic Identity",
          "summary": "G super Locrian and Ab melodic minor contain the same notes; practitioners use 'melodic minor built a half step above the altered dominant root' as the practical mnemonic.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "SymmetryMovement",
            "equivalence": "super_locrian_equals_melodic_minor_up_half_step"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 11,
              "topic": "Super Locrian formula and Ab melodic minor identity",
              "quote_excerpt": "Ab melodic minor scale contains the same notes as the G super Locrian scale",
              "page": 160
            },
            {
              "chapter_n": 6,
              "topic": "Bb melodic minor common scalar ground",
              "quote_excerpt": "scalar common ground between these chords, as they are, is basically the Bb melodic minor scale",
              "page": 62
            }
          ]
        }
      ],
      "modification_rules": [
        {
          "id": "lydian-dominant-4th-avoidance",
          "name": "Lydian Dominant: Avoid Natural 4th Against Dominant 3rd",
          "summary": "Over natural dominant seventh chords, the natural 4th of Mixolydian risks clashing with the chordal 3rd; Lydian dominant (raised 4th) eliminates this clash and adds #11.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "OmissionAllow",
            "omit_tone": "natural_4th",
            "replacement": "raised_4th",
            "applies_to": "v-dom7-natural"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 6,
              "topic": "Lydian dominant for dominant extensions",
              "quote_excerpt": "avoiding the natural 4th or chordal 11th, which could clash with the 3rd",
              "page": 55
            },
            {
              "chapter_n": 8,
              "topic": "Lydian dominant vs. Mixolydian",
              "quote_excerpt": "avoids the potential clash of the natural 4th sustained against the chord",
              "page": 93
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "scales-as-starting-point-ceiling",
          "name": "Scale-as-Method Ceiling: Scales Liberate Then Trap",
          "summary": "Scales are a starting point for note choice, not a complete method; treating any single scale as the total how-to for a chord creates a box of limitations.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "DensityCeiling",
            "ceiling_description": "no single scale exhausts note choice over any chord or progression"
          },
          "references": [
            {

              "topic": "Scales as starting point, not ceiling",
              "quote_excerpt": "scales do not tell the whole story when it comes to improvising\u2014not even about note choice",
              "page": 92
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "bebop-diminished-restraint",
          "name": "Bebop Dominant and Diminished Scales: Mastery + Restraint",

          "engine_payload": {

    {
      "id": "heussenstamm-silbergleit:goldmine-100-jazz-lessons:voicing-and-comping-texture",
      "name": "Voicing Density and Comping Texture System",
      "summary": "This system governs how chord voicings are constructed, selected, and texturally deployed in comping contexts. Members are voicing types ranging from full closed-position shapes through shell voicings (root + 3rd + 7th, then rootless two-string), quartal voicings, rootless extended voicings, and down to sparse guide-tone pairs or full silence (laying out). Traversal rules govern movement between voicing density levels: comping density is the primary dynamic lever, and the player moves from sparse guide tones upward through quartal and rootless to full textures based on musical context. Modification rules cover the dual root-string organizing principle (sixth-string or fifth-string root for minimal position shifts), the formula-based chord-family modification system (raise/lower 5th or 9th to alter; change 3rd quality to switch major/minor), the chord-melody rules (melody always on top; melody within a held shape), and the Freddie Green straight-4 constraints (precise release timing, minimal voicing size, felt-more-than-heard volume). The system explicitly warns against treating the default chord shape as a patch for slash chord notation.",
      "members": [
        {
          "id": "full-closed-voicing",
          "name": "Full Closed Voicing",
          "summary": "Complete chord with root on sixth or fifth string; all chord tones present; basis for movable-shape transposition."
        },
        {
          "id": "shell-voicing-rootless",
          "name": "Shell Voicing \u2014 Rootless (3rd + 7th)",
          "summary": "Two-string voicing on middle strings containing only the 3rd and 7th; root omitted; designed for use alongside a bassist."
        },
        {
          "id": "quartal-voicing",
          "name": "Quartal Voicing",
          "summary": "Stacked perfect 4ths, exploiting guitar's native string tuning; enables outside patterns and requires finger-rolling."
        },
        {
          "id": "rootless-extended-voicing",
          "name": "Rootless Extended Voicing",
          "summary": "Familiar chord shape with root on low string omitted; increases mobility; bass covers root in ensemble."
        },
        {
          "id": "guide-tone-sparse",
          "name": "Sparse Guide-Tone Comping",
          "summary": "Only 3rds and 7ths voiced; minimal density; maximum space for the soloist."
        },
        {
          "id": "laying-out",
          "name": "Laying Out",
          "summary": "Complete silence from the comper; an intentional texture choice in musical conversation."
        },
        {
          "id": "straight-4-voicing",
          "name": "Straight-4 / Freddie Green Voicing",
          "summary": "Quarter-note pulse with small root-3rd-7th shapes, precise release timing, minimal voicing size, low dynamic placement."
        }
      ],
      "traversal_rules": [
        {
          "id": "dual-root-string-proximity",
          "name": "Dual Root-String Organizing Rule",
          "summary": "Every chord is learned in two voicings \u2014 root on sixth string and root on fifth string \u2014 so the next chord in a progression is always nearby without large position shifts.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "PositionContinuity",
            "root_strings": [
              6,
              5
            ],
            "goal": "minimize_fretboard_shift"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 13,
              "topic": "Two chord forms per root",
              "quote_excerpt": "you can always have the next chord coming up in a progression close by",
              "page": 183
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "comping-density-as-dynamic-lever",
          "name": "Comping Density as Primary Dynamic Lever",
          "summary": "Move through voicing density levels (sparse guide tones \u2192 pentatonic ideas \u2192 full voicings \u2192 laying out) as the primary means of shaping intensity through a tune.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "TextureCycle",
            "levels": [
              "laying-out",
              "guide-tone-sparse",
              "shell-voicing-rootless",
              "quartal-voicing",
              "rootless-extended-voicing",
              "full-closed-voicing"
            ]
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 8,
              "topic": "Comping density as dynamic tool",
              "quote_excerpt": "vary the density, and with it the intensity, of our comping to fit different musical situations",
              "page": 94
            },
            {
              "chapter_n": 8,
              "topic": "Sparse comping: 3rds and 7ths only",
              "quote_excerpt": "if we want to allow the texture to be on the thin side, we could go as far as to lay out entirely",
              "page": 94
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "movable-shape-transposition",
          "name": "Movable Shape Transposition Across 12 Keys",
          "summary": "Any non-open-string voicing is moved fret by fret to transpose to other keys, exactly as barre chords are shifted.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "PositionContinuity",
            "mechanism": "fret_shift_transposition"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 13,
              "topic": "Movable chord shapes",
              "quote_excerpt": "all chord shapes are movable when you're not using open-string voicings",
              "page": 183
            }
          ]
        }
      ],
      "modification_rules": [
        {
          "id": "formula-modification-chord-family",
          "name": "Formula-Based Chord Modification Within and Across Families",
          "summary": "Starting from a dominant seventh formula (1-3-5-b7), raise or lower the 5th or 9th to alter; flatten the 3rd to shift to minor family; raise the 7th to shift to major family.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "SubstitutionExpand",
            "base_formula": "1-3-5-b7",
            "operations": [
              "raise_5th",
              "lower_5th",
              "raise_9th",
              "lower_9th",
              "flatten_3rd",
              "raise_7th"
            ]
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 13,
              "topic": "Chord system via formula modification",
              "quote_excerpt": "If we study the A7 chord and its formula, we can change it and create other chords",
              "page": 184
            },
            {
              "chapter_n": 13,
              "topic": "Altered chord construction",
              "quote_excerpt": "raise or lower the 5th or the 9th note by a half step",
              "page": 188
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "shell-rootless-density-reduction",
          "name": "Shell Voicing: Root Omission for Compactness",
          "summary": "Full chord voicings are reduced to 3rd + 7th by removing the root, producing compact two-string middle-string shapes suitable for use over a walking bass.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "OmissionAllow",
            "omit_tone": "root",
            "result": "shell-voicing-rootless",
            "condition": "bassist_present"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 5,
              "topic": "Two-note shell definition",
              "quote_excerpt": "leaving off the root, to boot ... very compact, easy-to-play, and flexible little two-string voicings",
              "page": 48
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "chord-melody-top-voice-rule",
          "name": "Chord Melody: Melody Always on Top",
          "summary": "In chord-melody playing the melody note must always be the highest-pitched note in the voicing.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "ColorToneRequire",
            "required_position": "highest_voice",
            "applies_to": "chord-melody"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 13,
              "topic": "Chord melody rule: melody on top",
              "quote_excerpt": "always have the melody of the tune or song on top of the chords as the highest sounding pitch",
              "page": 196
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "straight-4-voicing-constraints",
          "name": "Straight-4 (Freddie Green) Voicing Density Constraints",
          "summary": "Straight-4 comping requires minimal voicing size (root-3rd-7th or smaller), precise short release between quarter notes (not sustained, not chopped), and volume kept low ('felt more than heard').",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "DensityCeiling",
            "max_notes": 3,
            "release_timing": "swung_quarter_gap",
            "dynamic": "felt_more_than_heard"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 5,
              "topic": "Straight-4 voicing size",
              "quote_excerpt": "moderate or minimal in size, and some small root-3rd-and-7th kind of chord shapes",
              "page": 46
            },
            {
              "chapter_n": 5,
              "topic": "Straight-4 volume and context",
              "quote_excerpt": "'Felt more than heard' is the classic description of its role in the swing-era big band",
              "page": 47
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "partial-rootless-extension",
          "name": "Partial Voicings and Rootless Extended Chords",
          "summary": "Not all chord tones need be present; moving the root up a step generates ninth chords; rootless voicings are encouraged in ensemble contexts where the bass covers the root.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "OmissionAllow",
            "omit_tone": "root",
            "also_allows": "omit_any_non_guide_tone"

          "references": [
